name: Rambler Mine
short_description: High-elevation BLM rockhounding site in the Laramie Range. Find quartz and feldspar specimens on public land with minimal crowds and scenic mountain views.
description:
Rambler Mine sits at 9,713 feet in Albany County's high-elevation mineral zone, part of the Laramie Range batholith. This BLM-managed location offers personal-use rockhounding on relatively flat, accessible terrain for the altitude. The site is known for quartz specimens, feldspar crystals, and mica that weathered from granitic bedrock typical of the Archean-age core of the Laramie Mountains.

The geology here reflects Precambrian granite intrusions that formed deep underground and were later exposed by erosion. The high elevation keeps this site snow-covered November through April, but summer and early fall offer excellent collecting conditions. The relatively flat terrain makes this more accessible than many alpine rockhounding sites, though the altitude itself requires acclimatization.

This location sees moderate use compared to popular fee-dig sites, making it a good choice for rockhounders seeking solitude and unclaimed material. The BLM allows 25 pounds per day of personal-use collection with no permit required. Bring extra water, sun protection, and be prepared for afternoon thunderstorms in summer months.
history: The Rambler Mine area lies within the Laramie Range batholith, a massive Archean-age granite intrusion that crystallized approximately 2.8 billion years ago. The quartz and feldspar specimens found here formed as primary minerals in the cooling magma, later fractured and altered by uplift, weathering, and hydrothermal circulation that created the collectible crystal pockets exposed today.
tips: Search along eroded granite outcrops and talus slopes where weathering has exposed fresh quartz pockets and feldspar crystals - clear quartz points are common in small fractures. Use a small hammer and chisel to carefully extract specimens from the host rock without damaging crystals; many pieces here are fragile. Look for areas where recent rockfall has exposed new material, particularly on south-facing slopes. Bring a magnet to distinguish mica from feldspar, and watch for quartz's glassy luster versus the duller appearance of surrounding granite.
terrain_notes: At 9,713 feet elevation on relatively flat alpine terrain with scattered granite boulders and talus. Sparse vegetation typical of high alpine - low shrubs and tundra grasses. Walking distance from parking is typically 0.5 to 2 miles over rocky, uneven ground. Footing is stable but requires sturdy hiking boots due to loose rock and occasional steep micro-terrain between outcrops.
best_season: June - September. High elevation keeps the site snow-covered and inaccessible October through May. July and August offer the most stable weather, but afternoon thunderstorms are common. Early June and September can have snow patches on north-facing slopes.
written_directions: From Laramie, take WY 130 (Snowy Range Road) northwest toward Centennial for approximately 15 miles. Continue past the Snowy Range Ski Area turnoff. Look for BLM signage and pullouts near the Albany County mineral-collecting area around mile marker 18-20. The GPS coordinates point to a trailhead parking area; drive as far as road conditions allow, then hike to mineral exposures. Note: WY 130 closes in winter; confirm road status before visiting November through May.
road_conditions: WY 130 is paved in summer but may have washboard sections near turnoffs. High-clearance vehicle recommended for accessing remote pullouts. Road closed seasonally November through May depending on snow.
parking_notes: Primitive BLM pullouts along WY 130 corridor near the collecting area. No developed parking lot; parking limited to roadside turnouts with space for 3-5 vehicles. No facilities or water available.
collection_rules: Personal use only. Maximum 25 lbs per day per FLPMA regulations. No commercial collection. No motorized excavation or blasting. Collect only loose material and specimens that can be removed by hand tools. Do not damage or deface bedrock outcrops. Leave the site as you found it.
rules:

Maximum 25 lbs per day per FLPMA
No commercial collection
No motorized excavation
No blasting
Use hand tools only
Do not deface bedrock
Pack out all waste
nearest_services: Centennial, WY (small community, ~20 miles south) has limited services. Laramie (full services including gas, food, lodging, medical) is approximately 30 miles south via WY 130.
accessibility_notes: Moderate terrain: uneven rocky ground and talus slopes with elevation gain between outcrops. No wheelchair access. Sturdy hiking boots required. High elevation (9,713 ft) may cause altitude effects; acclimate before strenuous hiking. Not recommended for young children or those with limited mobility.

faq.question: What gemstones can you find rockhounding at Rambler Mine in Wyoming?
faq.answer: Rambler Mine is known for quartz specimens, feldspar crystals, and mica. Clear quartz points are the most common collectible, often found in small fractures within the granite bedrock. Feldspar crystals vary in size from small chips to collectible specimens. Mica books also occur and are popular for display.

faq.question: Do you need a permit to collect rocks at Rambler Mine?
faq.answer: No permit is required. Rambler Mine is located on BLM land in Wyoming, which allows personal-use rockhounding under FLPMA regulations. You can collect up to 25 pounds per day without applying for any permits.

faq.question: What is the best time of year to rockhound at Rambler Mine near Laramie?
faq.answer: June through September is the best rockhounding season. The site sits at 9,713 feet elevation and is snow-covered from October through May. July and August offer the most stable weather, though afternoon thunderstorms are common. Early June and late September may have lingering snow patches.

faq.question: How do you get to Rambler Mine from Laramie?
faq.answer: Take WY 130 (Snowy Range Road) northwest from Laramie toward Centennial for approximately 15 miles past the Snowy Range Ski Area. Look for BLM pullouts and signage near mile markers 18-20 in the Albany County mineral-collecting area. From the parking area, hike to exposed granite outcrops and talus slopes.

faq.question: Is Rambler Mine beginner-friendly for rockhounding?
faq.answer: Yes, Rambler Mine is suitable for beginners and families with children ages 8 and older. The relatively flat alpine terrain is easier than many mountain sites, and quartz crystals are common enough to give beginners good success. However, the high elevation and remote location mean you should be prepared with proper gear, water, and navigation.

faq.question: What tools and equipment do you need for rockhounding at Rambler Mine?
faq.answer: Bring a small hammer, cold chisel, and gently tap crystals free from host rock. Sturdy hiking boots are essential for rocky, uneven terrain at altitude. Pack extra water (at least 2-3 liters), sun protection, a small magnet to test for mica, and a collection bag. A GPS unit or map is helpful given the remote location and spotty cell service.

meta_description: Find quartz and feldspar at Rambler Mine on BLM land near Laramie. Personal use allowed, 25 lbs/day limit, no permit required. Alpine site, June-September.
